tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Install cert-manager via Helm and verify the installation. cert-manager is a prime example of the Operator pattern — it automatically issues and renews TLS certificates. After installation, confirm the controller Pods created in the cert-manager namespace and list the CRDs it registered.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Add the cert-manager Helm repository
helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io
helm repo update

# 2. Install cert-manager (including CRDs)
helm install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager \
  --namespace cert-manager \
  --create-namespace \
  --version v1.14.0 \
  --set installCRDs=true

# 3. Wait for all controller Pods to reach Running state
kubectl get pods -n ___

# 4. List CRDs registered by cert-manager
kubectl get crd | grep ___
tasks.solution:
# 1 & 2. Helm installation (run the commands above)

# 3. Check cert-manager Pods
kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
# NAME                                       READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# cert-manager-xxxx                          1/1     Running   0          60s
# cert-manager-cainjector-xxxx               1/1     Running   0          60s
# cert-manager-webhook-xxxx                  1/1     Running   0          60s

# 4. cert-manager CRD list
kubectl get crd | grep cert-manager
# NAME                                             CREATED AT
# certificaterequests.cert-manager.io              ...
# certificates.cert-manager.io                     ...
# challenges.acme.cert-manager.io                  ...
# clusterissuers.cert-manager.io                   ...
# issuers.cert-manager.io                          ...
# orders.acme.cert-manager.io                      ...
tasks.explanation: cert-manager is a textbook example of the Operator pattern. Installing it via Helm creates three controller Pods: cert-manager (core controller), cainjector (CA injector), and webhook (validation webhook). It also registers several CRDs including Certificate, Issuer, and ClusterIssuer. These CRDs serve as the Operator's domain-specific API.
hints: It may take 1–2 minutes for the Pods to start after helm install. Use kubectl get pods -n cert-manager -w to watch the state changes in real time.
Newer versions may use --set crds.enabled=true instead of installCRDs=true. Run helm show values jetstack/cert-manager to see the available options.
In an offline environment, configure a local registry with kind or k3d, or use OCI archives.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Create a SelfSigned Issuer and a Certificate custom resource for cert-manager, then observe the reconciliation loop as the controller automatically generates an actual TLS certificate (Secret). Once Certificate status shows Ready: True, inspect the tls.crt and tls.key fields of the created Secret.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create a SelfSigned Issuer
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Issuer
metadata:
  name: selfsigned-issuer
  namespace: default
spec:
  selfSigned: {}

---
# 2. Create a Certificate custom resource
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Certificate
metadata:
  name: my-tls-cert
  namespace: default
spec:
  secretName: my-tls-secret    # Name of the Secret to be created
  duration: 2160h               # 90 days
  dnsNames:
    - example.com
    - www.example.com
  issuerRef:
    name: ___                   # Name of the Issuer created above
    kind: Issuer

# 3. Watch Certificate status until Ready: True
kubectl get certificate my-tls-cert -w

# 4. Inspect the created Secret
kubectl get secret ___
kubectl describe secret ___
tasks.solution:
# Save and apply the files
kubectl apply -f issuer.yaml
# issuer.cert-manager.io/selfsigned-issuer created

kubectl apply -f certificate.yaml
# certificate.cert-manager.io/my-tls-cert created

# Watch the reconciliation loop (flips to Ready: True within seconds)
kubectl get certificate my-tls-cert -w
# NAME          READY   SECRET          AGE
# my-tls-cert   False   my-tls-secret   2s
# my-tls-cert   True    my-tls-secret   5s   <-- controller finished issuing

# Inspect the created Secret
kubectl get secret my-tls-secret
# NAME            TYPE                DATA   AGE
# my-tls-secret   kubernetes.io/tls   3      10s

kubectl describe secret my-tls-secret
# Data
# ====
# ca.crt:   xxxx bytes
# tls.crt:  xxxx bytes
# tls.key:  xxxx bytes

# Inspect certificate contents (base64 decode)
kubectl get secret my-tls-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -text -noout | grep -E 'Subject:|DNS:'
tasks.explanation: When a Certificate custom resource is created, the cert-manager controller runs the reconciliation loop. It compares the current state (no Secret, Ready: False) against the desired state (a valid TLS Secret, Ready: True) and issues a certificate and creates the Secret accordingly. This is the core of the Operator pattern: the user only describes *what* they want (the Certificate declaration), while the controller handles *how* to produce it (generating the CSR, signing it, and storing it as a Secret).
hints: Check the Ready state of a Certificate with kubectl get certificate -o wide or in the Conditions section of kubectl describe certificate my-tls-cert.
If certificate issuance fails, the reason will be recorded in the Events section of kubectl describe certificate.
The reconciliation loop also automatically triggers renewal when the certificate approaches expiry (at 2/3 of the duration). This is a key advantage over managing certificates manually.

tasks.prompt: [Application] Edit the existing Certificate to add 'api.example.com' to its dnsNames. After applying the change with kubectl edit or kubectl apply, observe the controller automatically re-issuing the certificate and updating the Secret. Compare the Subject Alternative Names (SANs) in tls.crt before and after the update.
tasks.starter:
# Option 1: edit directly with kubectl edit
kubectl edit certificate my-tls-cert
# Add 'api.example.com' to dnsNames and save

# Option 2: update the YAML and re-apply
# Edit the dnsNames section in certificate.yaml:
#   dnsNames:
#     - example.com
#     - www.example.com
#     - api.example.com    # added
# kubectl apply -f certificate.yaml

# Watch the update propagate
kubectl get certificate my-tls-cert -w

# Verify the SANs after renewal
kubectl get secret my-tls-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -text -noout | grep DNS
tasks.solution:
# Edit with kubectl edit (opens vi/nano)
kubectl edit certificate my-tls-cert
# Update the dnsNames section as follows, then save:
#   dnsNames:
#     - example.com
#     - www.example.com
#     - api.example.com

# The controller detects the change and begins re-issuance (briefly goes False, then True again)
kubectl get certificate my-tls-cert -w
# NAME          READY   SECRET          AGE
# my-tls-cert   True    my-tls-secret   2m
# my-tls-cert   False   my-tls-secret   2m   <-- re-issuance in progress
# my-tls-cert   True    my-tls-secret   2m   <-- re-issuance complete

# Verify the renewed certificate's SANs
kubectl get secret my-tls-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -text -noout | grep DNS
# DNS:example.com, DNS:www.example.com, DNS:api.example.com  <-- now 3 SANs
tasks.explanation: In the Operator pattern, the controller watches for changes to custom resources and automatically reconciles whenever the desired state diverges from the actual state. Detecting the dnsNames change, the cert-manager controller revokes the old certificate and re-issues a new one with the updated SANs. With a single YAML change, a complex PKI re-issuance process is fully automated. This is the core value of the Operator pattern — encoding operational knowledge in code.
hints: The save shortcut varies by editor. For vi use ':wq'; for nano use 'Ctrl+X → Y → Enter'.
Run kubectl describe certificate my-tls-cert immediately after the change to see in the Events section why the controller triggered re-issuance.
Clean up: kubectl delete certificate my-tls-cert && kubectl delete issuer selfsigned-issuer && kubectl delete secret my-tls-secret
